scenario: Catalyst Bank models a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process and groups the KYC steps into an embedded subprocess that runs across 12000 customers per month. The reviewer asks for the start event configuration of the embedded subprocess to comply with BPMN execution rules in Camunda 8.8.
question: Which start event configuration is valid inside the embedded subprocess in this Camunda 8.8 model?

options.text: Exactly one timer start event, optionally followed by additional timer start events used as alternative entry points to the embedded subprocess in the same diagram
One none start event together with one message start event so the subprocess can be entered either directly or by a correlated message in the same scope
Exactly one none start event, with no other start events of any type defined inside the embedded subprocess in this Camunda 8.8 diagram of the parent
One none start event together with one signal start event used to re-enter the subprocess after a broadcast signal from a different process instance in the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. The embedded subprocesses page states that an embedded subprocess must have exactly one start event, and that start event is required to be a none start event in the diagram.

b.text: Incorrect. Only one start event is allowed in an embedded subprocess, and it must be of type none; message start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess.

c.text: Correct. The embedded subprocesses page states that an embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event, and that other start events are explicitly not allowed inside the embedded subprocess.

d.text: Incorrect. Additional start events are not allowed inside an embedded subprocess; the rule is exactly one start event, and it must be of type none in the diagram.

explanation: Embedded subprocesses are constrained to a single none start event. When the subprocess is entered, that start event activates and the subprocess stays active until its last contained element completes, at which point the outgoing sequence flow is taken.
